summary: This post investigates whether prior meditation experience predicts success with jhanas, a series of altered mental states accessed through concentration. Analyzing data from 81 participants across Jhourney retreats, the author finds virtually no difference in jhana success rates between experienced and beginner meditators. While experienced meditators showed some tendency to progress further when successful, these differences were not statistically attributable to meditation experience itself. The author hypothesizes that jhana success depends on two underlying skills — the ability to invoke positive feelings and the ability to sustain attention — rather than meditation hours logged. She draws an analogy to Rich Sutton's 'bitter lesson' in AI, suggesting that brute-force practice of core skills may matter more than introspective understanding of one's mental barriers.
key_points: Data from 81 retreat participants shows no significant difference in jhana success rates between experienced and beginner meditators, regardless of how experience is measured

While experienced meditators showed some tendency to progress further through jhanic states, correlation analysis revealed no significant link between any measure of meditation experience and jhana skill
The author proposes that jhana success depends on two specific skills: the ability to invoke a positive feeling in the body and the ability to sustain attention
Popular meditation practices like mindfulness or Vipassana may develop mechanistically different skills than those needed for jhana access
Drawing on Sutton's 'bitter lesson' from AI, the author suggests brute-force cultivation of joy and attention may be more effective than deep introspection into psychological barriers
money_quotes: Across all three dimensions of meditation experience, we see virtually no difference in success rates between experienced vs. beginner meditators.
We found no correlation between meditation experience and any jhana skill.
We ought to exercise caution in describing jhanas as an 'advanced meditation practice,' because it could deter those who might otherwise succeed – and personally benefit – from the jhanas.

What does it mean to be an 'experienced' meditator, in the way that someone is an 'experienced' swimmer? Number of hours meditated tells us how long someone has been practicing, but not how adept they are at cultivating and sustaining a quiet mind.
Experienced meditators may have more confidence that they know what meditation is, so while they have assets, they are also at times mistaken about which skills to use and when.
Someone who practices violin for an hour a day is not necessarily proficient at the violin.

Instead of introspecting heavily – trying to model where these feelings came from, and how they impact one's behavior – it could be more effective to simply 'brute force' one's way into sparking joy and cultivating attention.
key_insight: Jhana success appears driven not by accumulated meditation experience but by two specific underlying skills — invoking positive feelings and sustaining attention — suggesting we should measure aptitude rather than practice hours.
